subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
coverage_standard: Expanded NCBE-aligned Criminal Law and Procedure rule bank covering homicide, other crimes, inchoate crimes and parties, general principles, and constitutional protections of accused persons.

rules.title: Common Law Murder
rules.prompt_question: What are the elements of common law murder?
rules.rule_statement: Common law murder is the unlawful killing of another human being with malice aforethought.
rules.application_example: If D intentionally shoots V without legal justification, D commits murder.
rules.common_trap: Malice aforethought does not require hatred.

rules.title: Malice Aforethought
rules.prompt_question: What mental states satisfy malice aforethought?
rules.rule_statement: Malice aforethought is satisfied by intent to kill, intent to inflict serious bodily injury, depraved-heart recklessness, or felony murder.
rules.application_example: Firing into a crowded room can show depraved-heart malice.
rules.common_trap: Ordinary negligence is not malice.

rules.title: Intended Killing
rules.prompt_question: When is an intended killing murder?
rules.rule_statement: An intentional killing without adequate mitigation, excuse, or justification is murder.
rules.application_example: D poisons V intending death and without provocation, resulting in murder.
rules.common_trap: Heat of passion may reduce the crime.

rules.title: Intent to Inflict Serious Bodily Injury
rules.prompt_question: When does intent to inflict serious bodily injury support murder?
rules.rule_statement: A killing caused by conduct intended to inflict serious bodily injury is murder even if death was not specifically intended.
rules.application_example: D beats V with a metal bat intending grave injury and V dies.
rules.common_trap: The intended injury must be serious, not trivial.

rules.title: Depraved-Heart Murder
rules.prompt_question: What is depraved-heart murder?
rules.rule_statement: Depraved-heart murder is a killing caused by reckless conduct manifesting extreme indifference to human life.
rules.application_example: D drives 100 mph through a crowded sidewalk area and kills a pedestrian.
rules.common_trap: This is more blameworthy than ordinary recklessness.

rules.title: Premeditation and Deliberation
rules.prompt_question: What is required for first-degree murder based on premeditation and deliberation?
rules.rule_statement: First-degree murder based on premeditation and deliberation requires a willful intent to kill formed after reflection, even if the reflection is brief.
rules.application_example: D pauses, re-aims, and then shoots V after deciding to kill.
rules.common_trap: Planning time can be very short.

rules.title: Enumerated First-Degree Murder
rules.prompt_question: What killings are often classified as first-degree murder by statute?
rules.rule_statement: Many statutes classify as first-degree murder killings committed by poison, lying in wait, torture, or during certain enumerated felonies.
rules.application_example: A killing by poison is often first-degree murder by statute.
rules.common_trap: Always check statutory language in the fact pattern.

rules.title: Voluntary Manslaughter
rules.prompt_question: When is murder reduced to voluntary manslaughter?
rules.rule_statement: Murder is reduced to voluntary manslaughter when committed in the heat of passion upon adequate provocation before reasonable cooling time and before actual cooling.
rules.application_example: D immediately kills after sudden violent attack by V.
rules.common_trap: Cooling off defeats the mitigation.

rules.title: Adequate Provocation
rules.prompt_question: What qualifies as adequate provocation?
rules.rule_statement: Adequate provocation is provocation that would arouse sudden and intense passion in an ordinary person, causing loss of self-control.
rules.application_example: Discovering a spouse in adultery traditionally qualifies.
rules.common_trap: Words alone usually do not qualify at common law.

rules.title: Imperfect Self-Defense
rules.prompt_question: What is imperfect self-defense?
rules.rule_statement: In some jurisdictions, an honest but unreasonable belief in the need for deadly self-defense reduces murder to voluntary manslaughter.
rules.application_example: D mistakenly but honestly thinks V is drawing a gun and kills V.
rules.common_trap: This doctrine is not universal.

rules.title: Involuntary Manslaughter
rules.prompt_question: What is involuntary manslaughter?
rules.rule_statement: Involuntary manslaughter is an unintentional killing resulting from criminal negligence or during an unlawful act not amounting to felony murder.
rules.application_example: D carelessly fires a gun into the air and kills someone below.
rules.common_trap: Civil negligence alone is usually insufficient.

rules.title: Misdemeanor Manslaughter
rules.prompt_question: What is misdemeanor manslaughter?
rules.rule_statement: An unintended killing during the commission of a misdemeanor or other unlawful act not qualifying for felony murder may constitute involuntary manslaughter.
rules.application_example: D commits unlawful battery and unexpectedly kills V.
rules.common_trap: The act must be unlawful and causally connected to the death.

rules.title: Felony Murder
rules.prompt_question: What is felony murder?
rules.rule_statement: Felony murder is a killing caused during the commission or attempted commission of a qualifying felony, without need to prove malice separately.
rules.application_example: A bystander dies during a bank robbery.
rules.common_trap: The underlying felony must usually be inherently dangerous and independent.

rules.title: BARRK Felonies
rules.prompt_question: Which felonies commonly support felony murder?
rules.rule_statement: Burglary, arson, rape, robbery, and kidnapping are the classic inherently dangerous felonies supporting felony murder.
rules.application_example: Death during kidnapping can trigger felony murder.
rules.common_trap: Not every felony qualifies.

rules.title: Merger Limitation
rules.prompt_question: What is the merger limitation?
rules.rule_statement: Felony murder does not apply when the underlying felony is an assaultive felony that is integral to the homicide.
rules.application_example: Aggravated battery causing death merges and cannot be the felony for felony murder.
rules.common_trap: This prevents bootstrapping every assault homicide into murder.

rules.title: Duration of Felony Murder Liability
rules.prompt_question: How long does felony murder liability last?
rules.rule_statement: Felony murder liability lasts through the felony and immediate flight until the felons reach a place of temporary safety.
rules.application_example: A killing during hot escape from robbery still counts.
rules.common_trap: Once temporary safety is reached, the rule generally ends.

rules.title: Killings by Third Parties
rules.prompt_question: When does a third-party killing support felony murder?
rules.rule_statement: Under the majority agency approach, felony murder applies only to killings by the felon or co-felon, while some jurisdictions use a proximate-cause approach for foreseeable third-party killings.
rules.application_example: Police killing a co-felon during robbery generally does not trigger felony murder under the agency rule.
rules.common_trap: The approach used is outcome-determinative.

rules.title: Causation in Homicide
rules.prompt_question: What causation is required for homicide?
rules.rule_statement: The defendant's act must be both the actual and proximate cause of death.
rules.application_example: If D stabs V and V later dies from infection, homicide causation may exist.
rules.common_trap: A superseding cause can cut off liability only if unforeseeable.

rules.title: Year-and-a-Day Rule
rules.prompt_question: What is the year-and-a-day rule?
rules.rule_statement: At common law, homicide liability required the victim to die within a year and a day of the defendant's act, though most jurisdictions have abolished the rule.
rules.application_example: A centuries-old common law rule would bar homicide if death occurred too late.
rules.common_trap: Usually tested as historical background.

rules.title: Larceny
rules.prompt_question: What are the elements of larceny?
rules.rule_statement: Larceny is the trespassory taking and carrying away of the tangible personal property of another with intent to permanently deprive.
rules.application_example: D grabs V's phone and keeps it.
rules.common_trap: Intent to return identical property later is still intent to steal.

rules.title: Custody Versus Possession
rules.prompt_question: Why does custody versus possession matter in theft crimes?
rules.rule_statement: If the victim gives the defendant mere custody and the defendant wrongfully converts the property, larceny may occur; if the defendant had lawful possession, embezzlement may apply.
rules.application_example: A store clerk usually has custody of store goods, not possession.
rules.common_trap: This distinction often decides between larceny and embezzlement.

rules.title: Continuing Trespass
rules.prompt_question: What is the continuing trespass doctrine?
rules.rule_statement: If the defendant takes property trespassorily without intent to permanently deprive but later forms that intent, the original trespass may continue so that larceny is established.
rules.application_example: D borrows a bike without permission intending to return it, then decides to keep it.
rules.common_trap: The initial taking must be wrongful.

rules.title: Larceny by Trick
rules.prompt_question: What is larceny by trick?
rules.rule_statement: Larceny by trick is obtaining possession of property by fraud with intent to permanently deprive, where title does not pass.
rules.application_example: D lies to borrow V's camera and intends to keep it.
rules.common_trap: If title passes, false pretenses is the better offense.

rules.title: Embezzlement
rules.prompt_question: What are the elements of embezzlement?
rules.rule_statement: Embezzlement is the fraudulent conversion of the personal property of another by a person already in lawful possession of that property.
rules.application_example: Treasurer diverts company funds to personal use.
rules.common_trap: Lawful possession must exist before the conversion.

rules.title: False Pretenses
rules.prompt_question: What are the elements of false pretenses?
rules.rule_statement: False pretenses is obtaining title to another's personal property by intentional false representation of a material past or present fact with intent to defraud.
rules.application_example: D lies about owning a painting and sells it to V.
rules.common_trap: Future promises usually are not enough absent present fraudulent intent.

rules.title: Receiving Stolen Property
rules.prompt_question: What are the elements of receiving stolen property?
rules.rule_statement: Receiving stolen property requires receiving possession and control of property known to be stolen by another, with intent to permanently deprive the owner.
rules.application_example: D buys a stolen laptop knowing it was stolen.
rules.common_trap: The property must actually be stolen by someone else.

rules.title: Robbery
rules.prompt_question: What are the elements of robbery?
rules.rule_statement: Robbery is larceny from the person or presence of the victim by force or intimidation.
rules.application_example: D snatches a purse after punching V.
rules.common_trap: Force used only after escape may be insufficient in some jurisdictions.

rules.title: From the Person or Presence
rules.prompt_question: What does 'from the person or presence' mean in robbery?
rules.rule_statement: Property is taken from the person or presence if it is taken from the victim's body or area of immediate control.
rules.application_example: Taking a wallet from a table beside V may still be robbery.
rules.common_trap: Actual physical contact with the victim is not required.

rules.title: Extortion
rules.prompt_question: What is extortion?
rules.rule_statement: Extortion is obtaining property by means of threats of future harm, unlike robbery which requires immediate force or intimidation.
rules.application_example: D demands money by threatening to reveal secrets next week.
rules.common_trap: Future threats point to extortion, not robbery.

rules.title: Common Law Burglary
rules.prompt_question: What are the elements of common law burglary?
rules.rule_statement: Common law burglary is the breaking and entering of the dwelling of another at night with intent to commit a felony therein.
rules.application_example: D opens a closed window at midnight and enters intending theft.
rules.common_trap: Modern burglary statutes are broader.

rules.title: Breaking
rules.prompt_question: What counts as a breaking for burglary?
rules.rule_statement: Breaking requires creating an opening by force, however slight, such as opening a closed door or window.
rules.application_example: Turning a doorknob on a closed but unlocked door can be a breaking.
rules.common_trap: Entering through an already open door is not a common law breaking.

rules.title: Constructive Breaking
rules.prompt_question: What is constructive breaking?
rules.rule_statement: Constructive breaking occurs when entry is gained by fraud, threat, or conspiracy rather than physical force.
rules.application_example: D tricks occupant into opening the door and then enters to steal.
rules.common_trap: This satisfies the breaking element at common law.

rules.title: Dwelling
rules.prompt_question: What is a dwelling for common law burglary?
rules.rule_statement: A dwelling is a structure regularly used for sleeping, together with outbuildings within the curtilage.
rules.application_example: A house used as a residence is a dwelling.
rules.common_trap: An abandoned building is not a dwelling.

rules.title: Common Law Arson
rules.prompt_question: What are the elements of common law arson?
rules.rule_statement: Common law arson is the malicious burning of the dwelling of another.
rules.application_example: D sets V's house on fire and blackens part of the structure.
rules.common_trap: Charring, not mere smoke damage, is required at common law.

rules.title: Charring Requirement
rules.prompt_question: What burning is required for common law arson?
rules.rule_statement: Arson requires material charring of the structure; mere discoloration or smoke damage is insufficient.
rules.application_example: Blackened structural wood satisfies the burning element.
rules.common_trap: Burning furniture alone is not enough unless modern statute expands the crime.

rules.title: Assault
rules.prompt_question: What is common law assault?
rules.rule_statement: Common law assault is either attempted battery or intentionally placing another in reasonable apprehension of imminent bodily harm.
rules.application_example: D swings and misses at V, creating attempted-battery assault.
rules.common_trap: Many statutes redefine assault.

rules.title: Battery
rules.prompt_question: What is common law battery?
rules.rule_statement: Common law battery is the unlawful application of force to the person of another resulting in bodily injury or offensive touching.
rules.application_example: D punches or spits on V.
rules.common_trap: Slight offensive contact is enough.

rules.title: Common Law Rape
rules.prompt_question: What are the elements of common law rape?
rules.rule_statement: Common law rape is unlawful sexual intercourse by a man with a woman not his wife by force or threat of force and without consent.
rules.application_example: D compels intercourse by threatening immediate serious harm.
rules.common_trap: Modern statutes broaden the offense well beyond this formulation.

rules.title: Statutory Rape
rules.prompt_question: What is statutory rape?
rules.rule_statement: Statutory rape is sexual intercourse with a person below the age of consent, and mistake as to age is often not a defense.
rules.application_example: Consensual intercourse with a minor under the statutory age can still be criminal.
rules.common_trap: Often strict liability as to age.

rules.title: Kidnapping
rules.prompt_question: What are the elements of kidnapping?
rules.rule_statement: Kidnapping is unlawful confinement plus movement of the victim or concealment in a secret place.
rules.application_example: D forces V into a trunk and drives away.
rules.common_trap: Movement incidental to another crime may be insufficient in some jurisdictions.

rules.title: Possession Offenses
rules.prompt_question: What is required for criminal possession?
rules.rule_statement: Possession may be actual or constructive and requires knowledge plus control for a sufficient time to terminate possession.
rules.application_example: D knows drugs are in a locked drawer to which only D has the key.
rules.common_trap: Mere proximity is not enough.

rules.title: Attempt
rules.prompt_question: What are the elements of attempt?
rules.rule_statement: Attempt requires specific intent to commit the target crime and an overt act that goes beyond preparation and constitutes a substantial step toward commission.
rules.application_example: D lies in wait outside the bank with weapon and mask.
rules.common_trap: Attempt usually requires specific intent even if the completed offense does not.

rules.title: Attempt Tests
rules.prompt_question: What tests are used to determine whether acts amount to attempt?
rules.rule_statement: Courts use tests such as substantial step, dangerous proximity, probable desistance, and unequivocality, but the MBE commonly emphasizes substantial step.
rules.application_example: Buying supplies alone may be preparation, while lying in wait is usually substantial step.
rules.common_trap: Know the distinction between preparation and attempt.

rules.title: Impossibility
rules.prompt_question: When is impossibility a defense to attempt?
rules.rule_statement: Factual impossibility is not a defense to attempt, but true legal impossibility may be.
rules.application_example: D tries to steal from an empty pocket and is still guilty of attempt.
rules.common_trap: Many supposed legal-impossibility fact patterns are really factual impossibility.

rules.title: Abandonment
rules.prompt_question: Is abandonment a defense to attempt?
rules.rule_statement: At common law, abandonment is not a defense once attempt is complete, though some modern statutes recognize voluntary and complete renunciation.
rules.application_example: D changes mind after taking a substantial step; common law still treats attempt as complete.
rules.common_trap: Fear of getting caught is not voluntary renunciation.

rules.title: Solicitation
rules.prompt_question: What are the elements of solicitation?
rules.rule_statement: Solicitation is asking, enticing, counseling, or commanding another to commit a crime with the intent that the crime be committed.
rules.application_example: D offers money to E to burn a building.
rules.common_trap: The crime is complete upon the request.

rules.title: Merger of Solicitation
rules.prompt_question: Does solicitation merge into other crimes?
rules.rule_statement: Solicitation merges into conspiracy if the person solicited agrees, and into the completed offense if the crime is accomplished.
rules.application_example: D's solicitation to rob merges into conspiracy once E agrees.
rules.common_trap: At common law, conspiracy does not merge into the completed offense.

rules.title: Conspiracy
rules.prompt_question: What are the elements of conspiracy?
rules.rule_statement: Conspiracy is an agreement between two or more persons to commit an unlawful act, with intent to agree and intent to achieve the criminal objective; many jurisdictions also require an overt act.
rules.application_example: D and E agree to rob a store and buy masks.
rules.common_trap: The agreement itself is the gist of the offense.

rules.title: Bilateral Versus Unilateral Conspiracy
rules.prompt_question: What is the difference between bilateral and unilateral conspiracy?
rules.rule_statement: Under the common law bilateral approach, at least two guilty minds must genuinely agree; under the MPC unilateral approach, one party's genuine agreement is sufficient even if the other only feigns agreement.
rules.application_example: If E is an undercover officer, conspiracy fails at common law but may succeed under MPC-based statutes.
rules.common_trap: The default MBE common law rule is usually bilateral unless statute suggests otherwise.

rules.title: Wharton's Rule
rules.prompt_question: What is Wharton's Rule?
rules.rule_statement: Wharton's Rule bars conspiracy liability when the target crime necessarily requires concerted criminal activity by the same number of persons involved, unless more persons participate than necessary.
rules.application_example: Only the two necessary parties to adultery may not also be guilty of conspiracy to commit adultery.
rules.common_trap: This rule is narrow and often displaced by statute.

rules.title: Pinkerton Liability
rules.prompt_question: When is a conspirator liable for crimes of co-conspirators?
rules.rule_statement: A conspirator is liable for crimes of co-conspirators committed in furtherance of the conspiracy, within its scope, and reasonably foreseeable.
rules.application_example: A foreseeable assault during a planned robbery can be imputed to all conspirators.
rules.common_trap: Withdrawal can cut off liability for future crimes.

rules.title: Withdrawal From Conspiracy
rules.prompt_question: How does a conspirator withdraw?
rules.rule_statement: Withdrawal requires affirmative notice to co-conspirators or law enforcement in time to allow abandonment, but it usually does not erase liability for the conspiracy already completed.
rules.application_example: D notifies police before the robbery occurs.
rules.common_trap: Withdrawal generally only cuts off future substantive liability.

rules.title: Principal
rules.prompt_question: Who is a principal?
rules.rule_statement: A principal is the person who personally commits the criminal act or uses an innocent instrumentality to do so.
rules.application_example: D tricks a child into delivering poison to V.
rules.common_trap: The mastermind can still be the principal even if another physically acts.

rules.title: Accomplice Liability
rules.prompt_question: What is required for accomplice liability?
rules.rule_statement: Accomplice liability requires assisting, encouraging, or facilitating the crime with the intent to aid and the intent that the principal commit the crime.
rules.application_example: D acts as lookout during burglary.
rules.common_trap: Mere presence or knowledge is insufficient.

rules.title: Additional Crimes of Principal
rules.prompt_question: When is an accomplice liable for additional crimes committed by the principal?
rules.rule_statement: An accomplice is liable for the intended offense and for other crimes that are the natural and probable consequences of the criminal venture.
rules.application_example: A robbery accomplice may be liable for a foreseeable assault during escape.
rules.common_trap: Liability depends on foreseeability and jurisdiction.

rules.title: Withdrawal by Accomplice
rules.prompt_question: How does an accomplice effectively withdraw?
rules.rule_statement: An accomplice withdraws by repudiating prior aid, attempting to neutralize assistance, and timely communicating withdrawal before the crime is committed.
rules.application_example: D retrieves the tools previously supplied for a burglary and warns the principal not to continue.
rules.common_trap: Withdrawal must occur before commission of the offense.

rules.title: Accessory After the Fact
rules.prompt_question: What is an accessory after the fact?
rules.rule_statement: An accessory after the fact knowingly assists a felon after completion of the felony in order to hinder apprehension, prosecution, or punishment.
rules.application_example: D hides a murderer in a basement to avoid arrest.
rules.common_trap: This is not participation in the underlying offense.

rules.title: Voluntary Act
rules.prompt_question: What act is required for criminal liability?
rules.rule_statement: Criminal liability requires a voluntary bodily movement or a qualifying omission where there is a legal duty to act.
rules.application_example: A reflex during sleep is not a voluntary act.
rules.common_trap: Status alone cannot be punished.

rules.title: Omissions
rules.prompt_question: When can an omission create liability?
rules.rule_statement: An omission creates criminal liability only when the defendant had a legal duty to act and was physically capable of acting.
rules.application_example: Parent fails to feed child despite ability to do so.
rules.common_trap: Moral duty alone is not enough.

rules.title: Sources of Legal Duty
rules.prompt_question: What are common sources of a legal duty to act?
rules.rule_statement: Legal duties arise from statute, contract, special relationship, voluntary assumption of care that secludes the victim, or creation of the peril.
rules.application_example: Lifeguard under contract must attempt rescue.
rules.common_trap: Know these categories cold.

rules.title: Required Mental State
rules.prompt_question: What mental states appear in criminal law?
rules.rule_statement: Criminal statutes may require purpose, knowledge, recklessness, negligence, malice, specific intent, general intent, or no mental state at all for strict liability crimes.
rules.application_example: A knowing-possession statute requires awareness of possession and the nature of the item.
rules.common_trap: Apply mens rea to each element carefully.

rules.title: MPC Mental States
rules.prompt_question: What are the MPC mental states?
rules.rule_statement: The MPC mental states are purpose, knowledge, recklessness, and negligence, ranked from most culpable to least culpable.
rules.application_example: If D is aware of and consciously disregards a substantial and unjustifiable risk, D acts recklessly.
rules.common_trap: If a statute is silent, the MPC often requires at least recklessness.

rules.title: Specific Intent Crimes
rules.prompt_question: What is a specific intent crime?
rules.rule_statement: A specific intent crime requires the defendant to act with a particular objective beyond the actus reus itself.
rules.application_example: Burglary requires entry with intent to commit a felony inside.
rules.common_trap: Specific intent crimes are especially vulnerable to mistake-of-fact and voluntary-intoxication defenses.

rules.title: Common Specific Intent Crimes
rules.prompt_question: Which crimes are commonly specific intent crimes?
rules.rule_statement: Common specific intent crimes include solicitation, attempt, conspiracy, first-degree premeditated murder, assault as attempted battery, larceny, robbery, burglary, embezzlement, false pretenses, and receiving stolen property.
rules.application_example: Because larceny is specific intent, an honest mistake of ownership can negate it.
rules.common_trap: Use mnemonic familiarity but still analyze elements.

rules.title: General Intent Crimes
rules.prompt_question: What is a general intent crime?
rules.rule_statement: A general intent crime requires only awareness of acting in the proscribed manner, without any further purpose.
rules.application_example: Battery often requires only intent to make the contact.
rules.common_trap: Unreasonable mistake of fact usually does not work here.

rules.title: Strict Liability
rules.prompt_question: What is a strict liability crime?
rules.rule_statement: A strict liability crime requires no mens rea as to one or more elements and commonly appears in public-welfare or statutory rape contexts.
rules.application_example: Selling contaminated food may be strict liability.
rules.common_trap: If the statute is silent and the penalty is severe, courts may resist finding strict liability.

rules.title: Mistake of Fact
rules.prompt_question: When is mistake of fact a defense?
rules.rule_statement: Mistake of fact is a defense if it negates the required mens rea; for specific intent crimes, the mistake need only be honest, while for general intent crimes it usually must be honest and reasonable.
rules.application_example: D honestly but unreasonably believes property is his and so may avoid larceny liability.
rules.common_trap: No defense for strict liability.

rules.title: Mistake of Law
rules.prompt_question: When is mistake of law a defense?
rules.rule_statement: Mistake of law is generally no defense unless the defendant reasonably relies on an official statement of law or the law itself makes knowledge of illegality an element.
rules.application_example: D relies on a court decision later reversed.
rules.common_trap: Advice of private counsel is usually not enough by itself.

rules.title: M'Naghten Test
rules.prompt_question: What is the M'Naghten insanity test?
rules.rule_statement: Under M'Naghten, the defendant is insane if, because of mental disease or defect, the defendant did not know the nature and quality of the act or did not know it was wrong.
rules.application_example: D thinks he is squeezing a lemon rather than strangling a person.
rules.common_trap: Moral insanity alone is not enough.

rules.title: Irresistible Impulse
rules.prompt_question: What is the irresistible impulse test?
rules.rule_statement: Under this test, a defendant is insane if mental illness prevented self-control and made the defendant unable to conform conduct to the law.
rules.application_example: D knew act was wrong but could not control himself due to disease.
rules.common_trap: Not all jurisdictions recognize this test.

rules.title: MPC Insanity Test
rules.prompt_question: What is the MPC insanity test?
rules.rule_statement: Under the MPC, the defendant is insane if, because of mental disease or defect, the defendant lacked substantial capacity either to appreciate the criminality of conduct or to conform conduct to the law.
rules.application_example: Severe psychosis can satisfy the standard if it significantly impairs cognition or control.
rules.common_trap: More lenient than M'Naghten.

rules.title: Diminished Capacity
rules.prompt_question: What is diminished capacity?
rules.rule_statement: Diminished capacity allows evidence of mental abnormality short of insanity to negate a specific-intent element in jurisdictions recognizing the doctrine.
rules.application_example: Mental impairment may negate premeditation.
rules.common_trap: Not a full excuse and not universal.

rules.title: Voluntary Intoxication
rules.prompt_question: When is voluntary intoxication a defense?
rules.rule_statement: Voluntary intoxication is a defense only if it negates specific intent; it is no defense to general intent, malice, recklessness, or strict liability crimes.
rules.application_example: D is too intoxicated to form intent to steal and so may defeat burglary.
rules.common_trap: It does not negate depraved-heart murder.

rules.title: Involuntary Intoxication
rules.prompt_question: When is involuntary intoxication a defense?
rules.rule_statement: Involuntary intoxication is a defense if it renders the defendant unable to appreciate criminality or conform conduct, similar to insanity.
rules.application_example: D is unknowingly drugged and later acts while severely impaired.
rules.common_trap: The intoxication must truly be involuntary.

rules.title: Self-Defense
rules.prompt_question: When may a defendant use self-defense?
rules.rule_statement: A person may use reasonable nondeadly force when reasonably necessary to defend against imminent unlawful force, and deadly force when reasonably necessary to prevent imminent death or serious bodily harm.
rules.application_example: D shoots only after V points a loaded gun.
rules.common_trap: Force must be proportional.

rules.title: Initial Aggressor
rules.prompt_question: When may an initial aggressor claim self-defense?
rules.rule_statement: An initial aggressor regains self-defense only by complete withdrawal communicated to the victim or if the victim escalates suddenly to deadly force.
rules.application_example: D starts a shove fight but V suddenly pulls a knife.
rules.common_trap: Aggressor status matters a lot.

rules.title: Retreat
rules.prompt_question: When is retreat required?
rules.rule_statement: At common law, retreat before using deadly force is required if safe retreat is known and feasible, except generally in one's own home.
rules.application_example: On a public street D may need to retreat if safely possible.
rules.common_trap: No retreat usually required for nondeadly force.

rules.title: Defense of Others
rules.prompt_question: When may force be used in defense of others?
rules.rule_statement: A defendant may use force in defense of others when the defendant reasonably believes the defended person may lawfully use such force.
rules.application_example: D tackles attacker to protect friend.
rules.common_trap: Jurisdictions differ on whether the defended person's right must actually exist.

rules.title: Defense of Property
rules.prompt_question: What force may be used to protect property?
rules.rule_statement: Reasonable nondeadly force may be used to prevent unlawful interference with property, but deadly force may not be used solely to protect property.
rules.application_example: D may shove trespasser out after warning if feasible.
rules.common_trap: Spring guns and deadly traps are generally forbidden.

rules.title: Necessity
rules.prompt_question: What is the necessity defense?
rules.rule_statement: Necessity applies when the defendant reasonably believes criminal conduct is necessary to prevent a greater imminent harm and there is no reasonable legal alternative.
rules.application_example: D breaks into cabin during blizzard to avoid freezing.
rules.common_trap: Traditionally unavailable for intentional homicide.

rules.title: Duress
rules.prompt_question: What is duress?
rules.rule_statement: Duress is a defense when the defendant commits a crime due to a threat of imminent death or serious bodily harm that would cause a reasonable person to yield.
rules.application_example: D drives getaway car with gun at head.
rules.common_trap: Traditionally no defense to intentional murder.

rules.title: Consent
rules.prompt_question: When is consent a defense in criminal law?
rules.rule_statement: Consent is generally not a defense to crimes involving breach of the peace or serious bodily injury, but may negate elements of some offenses such as larceny.
rules.application_example: Owner's consent defeats theft.
rules.common_trap: Victim cannot consent to serious criminal injury in many contexts.

rules.title: Entrapment
rules.prompt_question: What is entrapment?
rules.rule_statement: Entrapment exists when police induce a crime that the defendant was not predisposed to commit.
rules.application_example: Repeated government pressure creates a crime the defendant otherwise would not commit.
rules.common_trap: Opportunity alone is not entrapment.

rules.title: Causation
rules.prompt_question: What causation is required in result crimes?
rules.rule_statement: Result crimes require the defendant's conduct to be both the actual cause and the proximate cause of the prohibited result.
rules.application_example: Arson causing death requires causal link from fire to death.
rules.common_trap: Intervening causes matter only in result crimes.

rules.title: Search Definition
rules.prompt_question: What is a Fourth Amendment search?
rules.rule_statement: A search occurs when the government intrudes on a reasonable expectation of privacy or physically trespasses on a constitutionally protected area to obtain information.
rules.application_example: Police entering a home to gather evidence is a search.
rules.common_trap: Open fields are not protected like curtilage.

rules.title: Seizure Definition
rules.prompt_question: What is a Fourth Amendment seizure of a person?
rules.rule_statement: A person is seized when a reasonable person would not feel free to leave due to government force or show of authority, and the person submits or is physically restrained.
rules.application_example: An officer blocking D's path and ordering D to stop can create a seizure if D submits.
rules.common_trap: Mere police questioning alone is not always a seizure.

rules.title: Expectation of Privacy in Home
rules.prompt_question: What privacy protection exists in the home?
rules.rule_statement: The home receives the highest Fourth Amendment protection, and warrantless entry is presumptively unreasonable absent a recognized exception.
rules.application_example: Police ordinarily need a warrant to enter and search a house.
rules.common_trap: The area immediately surrounding the home, or curtilage, is also strongly protected.

rules.title: Open Fields and Curtilage
rules.prompt_question: How are open fields and curtilage treated?
rules.rule_statement: Curtilage is treated like the home for Fourth Amendment purposes, while open fields are not protected even if privately owned.
rules.application_example: Police walking through a distant rural field usually conduct no search, but entering the fenced backyard may be a search.
rules.common_trap: Property law concepts are not always controlling.

rules.title: Abandoned Property
rules.prompt_question: Does the Fourth Amendment protect abandoned property?
rules.rule_statement: A person has no reasonable expectation of privacy in property that has been abandoned.
rules.application_example: D throws drugs into a public trash bin while fleeing.
rules.common_trap: Abandonment is judged objectively from conduct.

rules.title: Warrant Requirements
rules.prompt_question: What is required for a valid warrant?
rules.rule_statement: A warrant must be issued by a neutral and detached magistrate, supported by probable cause, and describe with particularity the place to be searched and items or persons to be seized.
rules.application_example: A warrant for Apartment 3B and stolen jewelry is sufficiently particular.
rules.common_trap: General exploratory warrants are invalid.

rules.title: Probable Cause for Search
rules.prompt_question: What is probable cause to search?
rules.rule_statement: Probable cause to search exists when there is a fair probability that evidence or contraband will be found in the place to be searched.
rules.application_example: Reliable informant plus corroboration may establish probable cause to search a home.
rules.common_trap: Probable cause to arrest is distinct from probable cause to search a place.

rules.title: Probable Cause for Arrest
rules.prompt_question: What is probable cause to arrest?
rules.rule_statement: Probable cause to arrest exists when facts known to police would warrant a prudent person in believing the suspect committed or is committing a crime.
keywords: committed or is committing a crime
rules.application_example: Officer sees D selling bags of white powder in a hand-to-hand transaction.
rules.common_trap: Anonymous tip alone may be too weak without corroboration.

rules.title: Arrest in Public
rules.prompt_question: When may police arrest in public without a warrant?
rules.rule_statement: Police may make a warrantless public arrest if they have probable cause to believe the suspect committed a felony, and for misdemeanors committed in the officer's presence.
rules.application_example: Officer arrests D on the street based on probable cause for robbery.
rules.common_trap: Home arrests are more restricted.

rules.title: Arrest in Suspect's Home
rules.prompt_question: What is needed to arrest a suspect in the suspect's home?
rules.rule_statement: Absent consent or exigency, police need an arrest warrant to enter the suspect's own home to make a routine felony arrest.
rules.application_example: Police cannot simply walk into D's house to arrest D without warrant or exception.
rules.common_trap: This is distinct from entering a third party's home.

rules.title: Entry Into Third Party's Home
rules.prompt_question: What is required to enter a third party's home to arrest a suspect?
rules.rule_statement: Police generally need a search warrant for the third party's home, in addition to an arrest warrant for the suspect or another applicable exception.
rules.application_example: Police need more than just D's arrest warrant to enter E's home searching for D.
rules.common_trap: The third party's privacy rights are separately protected.

rules.title: Search Incident to Arrest
rules.prompt_question: What may be searched incident to a lawful arrest?
rules.rule_statement: Police may search the arrestee's person and the area within the arrestee's immediate control incident to a lawful custodial arrest.
rules.application_example: Officer searches D's pockets and backpack within reach during arrest.
rules.common_trap: No lawful arrest means no search-incident exception.

rules.title: Vehicle Search Incident to Arrest
rules.prompt_question: When may police search a vehicle incident to arrest?
rules.rule_statement: Police may search a vehicle incident to arrest if the arrestee is unsecured and within reaching distance of the passenger compartment, or if it is reasonable to believe evidence of the offense of arrest might be found inside.
rules.application_example: After arrest for drug possession, officers may search areas where drug evidence may be found.
rules.common_trap: Automatic vehicle search incident to every arrest is wrong.

rules.title: Automobile Exception
rules.prompt_question: When does the automobile exception apply?
rules.rule_statement: If police have probable cause to believe a vehicle contains contraband or evidence, they may search any area of the vehicle and containers that could contain the object of the search.
rules.application_example: Probable cause for rifle allows search of trunk but not a tiny coin purse.
rules.common_trap: Scope depends on the object sought.

rules.title: Plain View
rules.prompt_question: When can police seize evidence in plain view?
rules.rule_statement: Police may seize evidence in plain view if they are lawfully present, have lawful access, and its incriminating character is immediately apparent.
rules.application_example: During lawful entry, officer sees illegal gun on table.
rules.common_trap: Plain view does not justify entry in the first place.

rules.title: Consent Search
rules.prompt_question: When is consent search valid?
rules.rule_statement: A consent search is valid if consent is voluntary and given by someone with actual or apparent authority.
rules.application_example: Co-occupant consents to search shared living room.
rules.common_trap: A physically present co-occupant's express refusal controls against police entry as to common areas.

rules.title: Scope of Consent
rules.prompt_question: How is the scope of consent determined?
rules.rule_statement: Scope of consent is measured by objective reasonableness: what a typical reasonable person would have understood from the exchange.
rules.application_example: Consent to search for a gun includes spaces where a gun could fit.
rules.common_trap: Consent may be limited or revoked.

rules.title: Terry Stop and Frisk
rules.prompt_question: When may police stop and frisk?
rules.rule_statement: Police may briefly stop a person based on reasonable suspicion of criminal activity and frisk for weapons based on reasonable suspicion the person is armed and dangerous.
rules.application_example: Officer sees suspicious bulge and casing behavior outside store.
rules.common_trap: A frisk is for weapons, not general evidence.

rules.title: Protective Sweep of Vehicle During Terry Stop
rules.prompt_question: When may police search the passenger compartment during a Terry stop?
rules.rule_statement: Police may search the passenger compartment for weapons during a lawful stop if they reasonably believe the suspect is dangerous and may gain immediate control of weapons.
rules.application_example: Officer may inspect car's reachable areas for a gun during roadside stop.
rules.common_trap: This is narrower than the automobile exception.

rules.title: Checkpoints
rules.prompt_question: When are suspicionless checkpoints valid?
rules.rule_statement: Suspicionless checkpoints are generally invalid for ordinary crime control but may be valid for special needs such as border security or sobriety checkpoints.
rules.application_example: A DUI checkpoint may be valid; a narcotics checkpoint aimed at general crime control is not.
rules.common_trap: Primary programmatic purpose matters.

rules.title: Inventory Searches
rules.prompt_question: When is an inventory search valid?
rules.rule_statement: Police may conduct a routine inventory search of lawfully impounded property pursuant to standardized procedures.
rules.application_example: After impounding a car, police inventory contents according to department policy.
rules.common_trap: Inventory cannot be a pretext for investigatory rummaging.

rules.title: Special Needs Searches
rules.prompt_question: What are special-needs searches?
rules.rule_statement: Searches conducted for needs beyond ordinary law enforcement may be reasonable without individualized suspicion under balancing analysis.
rules.application_example: Drug testing of certain safety-sensitive employees may be upheld.
rules.common_trap: General crime control is not a special need.

rules.title: Exigent Circumstances
rules.prompt_question: When may police act without a warrant due to exigent circumstances?
rules.rule_statement: Warrantless action is allowed when urgent needs such as hot pursuit, risk to safety, or imminent destruction of evidence make waiting unreasonable.
rules.application_example: Police chase armed suspect into house seconds after offense.
rules.common_trap: The exigency must be genuine.

rules.title: School and Workplace Searches
rules.prompt_question: What standards apply to some public school and government workplace searches?
rules.rule_statement: Certain public school and government workplace searches may be judged by reasonableness rather than probable cause and warrant requirements.
rules.application_example: A school official may search student bag on reasonable grounds.
rules.common_trap: These are special-context exceptions.

rules.title: Exclusionary Rule
rules.prompt_question: What is the exclusionary rule?
rules.rule_statement: Evidence obtained in violation of the Constitution may be excluded from the prosecution's case-in-chief, along with derivative evidence, unless an exception applies.
rules.application_example: Unlawfully seized drugs may be suppressed.
rules.common_trap: Not all constitutional violations trigger exclusion.

rules.title: Fruit of the Poisonous Tree
rules.prompt_question: What is fruit of the poisonous tree?
rules.rule_statement: Derivative evidence obtained as a result of unconstitutional government conduct is generally excluded unless sufficiently attenuated, independently sourced, or inevitably discoverable.
rules.application_example: Illegal entry leading police to hidden drugs and then to a confession may taint both.
rules.common_trap: Focus on causal connection and exceptions.

rules.title: Good-Faith Exception
rules.prompt_question: When does the good-faith exception apply?
rules.rule_statement: Evidence need not be excluded when police reasonably rely in good faith on a warrant, statute, or binding precedent later found defective, unless reliance was objectively unreasonable.
rules.application_example: Officers rely on a warrant signed by magistrate that later lacks probable cause.
rules.common_trap: No good faith if affidavit was knowingly false or warrant facially deficient.

rules.title: Independent Source and Inevitable Discovery
rules.prompt_question: What are the independent source and inevitable discovery doctrines?
rules.rule_statement: Evidence is admissible if later obtained from an independent lawful source or if the prosecution proves it would inevitably have been discovered lawfully.
rules.application_example: Search team later obtains valid warrant based on untainted information.
rules.common_trap: Inevitable discovery requires more than speculation.

rules.title: Standing to Challenge Search
rules.prompt_question: Who may challenge a search under the Fourth Amendment?
rules.rule_statement: Only a person whose own reasonable expectation of privacy was violated may seek suppression under the Fourth Amendment.
rules.application_example: Overnight house guest may challenge unlawful search of host's home.
rules.common_trap: Vicarious standing does not exist.

rules.title: Miranda Warnings
rules.prompt_question: When are Miranda warnings required?
rules.rule_statement: Miranda warnings are required before custodial interrogation by known government agents.
rules.application_example: Stationhouse questioning of handcuffed suspect requires warnings.
rules.common_trap: No custody or no interrogation means no Miranda requirement.

rules.title: Custody for Miranda
rules.prompt_question: What is custody for Miranda purposes?
rules.rule_statement: Custody exists when a reasonable person in the suspect's position would not feel free to terminate questioning and leave.
rules.application_example: Formal arrest is custody; roadside traffic stop usually is not full Miranda custody.
rules.common_trap: Custody here is not identical to seizure doctrine in every detail.

rules.title: Interrogation for Miranda
rules.prompt_question: What counts as interrogation under Miranda?
rules.rule_statement: Interrogation includes express questioning and words or actions police should know are reasonably likely to elicit an incriminating response.
rules.application_example: Officer's deliberate guilt-provoking speech may be interrogation even without direct questions.
rules.common_trap: Routine booking questions are generally excluded.

rules.title: Waiver of Miranda Rights
rules.prompt_question: What is required for a valid Miranda waiver?
rules.rule_statement: A Miranda waiver must be knowing, voluntary, and intelligent.
rules.application_example: D reads and signs waiver after receiving warnings and understanding them.
rules.common_trap: Coercive interrogation can undermine voluntariness.

rules.title: Invocation of Silence
rules.prompt_question: What happens if the suspect invokes the right to remain silent?
rules.rule_statement: If the suspect unambiguously invokes the right to remain silent, police must scrupulously honor the invocation before attempting renewed questioning.
rules.application_example: D says, 'I don't want to talk.'
rules.common_trap: Ambiguous statements may not suffice.

rules.title: Invocation of Counsel Under Miranda
rules.prompt_question: What happens if the suspect invokes the Miranda right to counsel?
rules.rule_statement: If the suspect unambiguously requests counsel, interrogation must cease until counsel is present, unless the suspect reinitiates communication or there is a sufficient break in custody.
rules.application_example: D says, 'I want a lawyer.'
rules.common_trap: This right is broader during custody than the Sixth Amendment offense-specific right.

rules.title: Public Safety Exception
rules.prompt_question: What is the public safety exception to Miranda?
rules.rule_statement: Police may ask questions reasonably prompted by immediate concern for public safety without first giving Miranda warnings.
rules.application_example: Officer asks where hidden gun is in crowded supermarket.
rules.common_trap: Exception is narrow and tied to immediate danger.

rules.title: Voluntariness of Confessions
rules.prompt_question: When is a confession involuntary under due process?
rules.rule_statement: A confession is involuntary if police coercion, viewed under the totality of circumstances, overbears the defendant's will.
rules.application_example: Threatening D's family to force confession makes confession involuntary.
rules.common_trap: Police coercion is generally required.

rules.title: Attachment of Sixth Amendment Right to Counsel
rules.prompt_question: When does the Sixth Amendment right to counsel attach?
rules.rule_statement: The Sixth Amendment right to counsel attaches when formal adversarial judicial proceedings begin, such as by formal charge, indictment, information, arraignment, or preliminary hearing.
rules.application_example: Once indicted, D has a Sixth Amendment right to counsel on that offense.
rules.common_trap: Pre-charge custodial interrogation is governed by Miranda, not the Sixth Amendment.

rules.title: Deliberate Elicitation
rules.prompt_question: What violates the Sixth Amendment right to counsel after attachment?
rules.rule_statement: After attachment, police may not deliberately elicit incriminating statements from the defendant about the charged offense without counsel or valid waiver.
rules.application_example: Police send informant to question indicted defendant in jail about the charged robbery.
rules.common_trap: This differs from Miranda interrogation doctrine.

rules.title: Offense-Specific Nature
rules.prompt_question: What does it mean that the Sixth Amendment right is offense specific?
rules.rule_statement: The Sixth Amendment right to counsel applies only to the offense formally charged and same-offense equivalents under Blockburger, not to unrelated uncharged offenses.
rules.application_example: D represented on assault charge may still be questioned about unrelated burglaries, subject to Miranda.
rules.common_trap: Do not confuse with Miranda counsel invocation.

rules.title: Post-Charge Lineups
rules.prompt_question: When is counsel required at an identification procedure?
rules.rule_statement: After the Sixth Amendment right attaches, the defendant has a right to counsel at post-charge physical lineups and showups.
rules.application_example: Indicted suspect in physical lineup must have counsel or valid waiver.
rules.common_trap: No Sixth Amendment right at photo arrays.

rules.title: Suggestive Identification Procedures
rules.prompt_question: When does an identification violate due process?
rules.rule_statement: An identification procedure violates due process if it is unnecessarily suggestive and creates a substantial likelihood of misidentification.
rules.application_example: Police tell witness that the suspect is number 3 in lineup.
rules.common_trap: Reliability can save an identification despite suggestiveness.

rules.title: Independent Basis for In-Court Identification
rules.prompt_question: When may an in-court identification still be allowed?
rules.rule_statement: An in-court identification may be admitted if it rests on an independent basis apart from the unconstitutional lineup or showup.
rules.application_example: Witness had long close view of perpetrator during crime.
rules.common_trap: Look to opportunity to observe, certainty, and timing factors.

rules.title: Attachment of Jeopardy
rules.prompt_question: When does jeopardy attach?
rules.rule_statement: Jeopardy attaches in jury trials when the jury is sworn and in bench trials when the first witness is sworn.
rules.application_example: After jury is sworn, acquittal bars retrial.
rules.common_trap: Filing charges alone is not enough.

rules.title: Same Offense
rules.prompt_question: How is the same offense determined?
rules.rule_statement: Under Blockburger, offenses are different if each requires proof of a fact the other does not; lesser-included offenses are generally the same offense as the greater offense.
rules.application_example: Conviction of robbery usually bars later prosecution for larceny arising from same act if larceny is lesser-included.
rules.common_trap: Same transaction is not the federal constitutional test.

rules.title: Multiple Punishments
rules.prompt_question: When does double jeopardy bar multiple punishments?
rules.rule_statement: Double jeopardy bars multiple punishments for the same offense unless the legislature clearly intended cumulative punishment.
rules.application_example: Court asks whether legislature intended separate punishment for felony and enhancement.
rules.common_trap: Legislative intent can authorize cumulative penalties.

rules.title: Exceptions to Double Jeopardy Bar
rules.prompt_question: What are key exceptions allowing retrial?
rules.rule_statement: Retrial is generally allowed after mistrial for hung jury, defendant's successful appeal not based on insufficient evidence, or separate-sovereigns prosecutions.
rules.application_example: Federal prosecution can follow state prosecution for same conduct under separate-sovereigns doctrine.
rules.common_trap: Acquittal usually ends the matter completely.

rules.title: Right to Jury Trial
rules.prompt_question: When is there a right to jury trial?
rules.rule_statement: A defendant has a jury-trial right for serious offenses, generally those punishable by more than six months' imprisonment.
rules.application_example: A one-year misdemeanor entitles D to jury trial.
rules.common_trap: Petty offenses do not.

rules.title: Burden of Proof
rules.prompt_question: What burden does the prosecution carry?
rules.rule_statement: The prosecution must prove every element of the offense beyond a reasonable doubt.
rules.application_example: State must prove intent and causation in murder case.
rules.common_trap: Elements cannot be shifted to defendant.

rules.title: Guilty Pleas
rules.prompt_question: What is required for a valid guilty plea?
rules.rule_statement: A guilty plea must be voluntary, knowing, and intelligent, with understanding of the charge, major consequences, and rights waived.
rules.application_example: Judge advises D of right to jury trial and nature of charge before plea.
rules.common_trap: Coerced or uninformed pleas can be set aside.

rules.title: Effective Assistance of Counsel
rules.prompt_question: What is required to prove ineffective assistance of counsel?
rules.rule_statement: The defendant must show deficient performance by counsel and a reasonable probability that, but for the deficiency, the result would have been different.
rules.application_example: Counsel's failure to investigate a solid alibi may satisfy both prongs.
rules.common_trap: Strategic choices are heavily deferred to.

rules.title: Speedy Trial
rules.prompt_question: What determines whether the speedy-trial right was violated?
rules.rule_statement: Courts balance length of delay, reason for delay, defendant's assertion of the right, and prejudice to the defendant.
rules.application_example: A long unjustified delay that harms the defense may violate the speedy-trial right.
rules.common_trap: The remedy is dismissal with prejudice.

rules.title: Public Trial
rules.prompt_question: What does the public-trial right protect?
rules.rule_statement: The Sixth Amendment generally guarantees a public criminal trial, though limited closures may be allowed if justified by overriding interests and narrowly tailored.
rules.application_example: Court cannot close courtroom without adequate findings.
rules.common_trap: Closures require careful justification.

rules.title: Confrontation Clause
rules.prompt_question: What does the Confrontation Clause prohibit?
rules.rule_statement: In criminal cases, testimonial hearsay by an unavailable witness is inadmissible against the defendant unless the defendant had a prior opportunity to cross-examine the witness.
rules.application_example: Prosecutor cannot introduce police-interview statement of absent witness without prior cross-examination opportunity.
rules.common_trap: Nontestimonial hearsay is governed mainly by hearsay rules, not Crawford.

rules.title: Compulsory Process
rules.prompt_question: What does compulsory process protect?
rules.rule_statement: The defendant has the right to obtain witnesses and evidence in the defendant's favor through compulsory process, subject to ordinary evidentiary rules.
rules.application_example: Court generally must permit subpoena of relevant defense witness.
rules.common_trap: The right is not absolute if evidence is properly excluded under neutral rules.

rules.title: Brady Material
rules.prompt_question: What must the prosecution disclose under Brady?
rules.rule_statement: The prosecution must disclose favorable material exculpatory and impeachment evidence in its possession.
rules.application_example: State must disclose witness deal undermining credibility.
rules.common_trap: Suppression can be implicit even without bad faith.

rules.title: Cruel and Unusual Punishment
rules.prompt_question: What does the Eighth Amendment prohibit?
rules.rule_statement: The Eighth Amendment prohibits cruel and unusual punishment, including certain barbaric methods and, in rare cases, grossly disproportionate sentences.
rules.application_example: Torturous punishment methods are unconstitutional.
rules.common_trap: Noncapital proportionality challenges are hard to win.

rules.title: Death Penalty Limits
rules.prompt_question: What are major constitutional limits on capital punishment?
rules.rule_statement: The death penalty may not be imposed for crimes against individuals where the victim was not killed, nor on intellectually disabled defendants or offenders under 18 at the time of the crime.
rules.application_example: No death penalty for rape of adult where victim lives.
rules.common_trap: Capital sentencing must also permit individualized mitigation.
